articles.title: Supreme Court Begins Answering Lingering Questions About Constitutional Constraints on Gun Control
articles.content:
上月在12天的时间里，最高法院支持了大麻消费者的持枪权利，驳回了夏威夷关于公共场所私有财产枪支的默认规则，并同意审理“突击武器”禁令的合宪性问题。这一系列第二修正案活动凸显了一个观点：那些看似合理的枪支管制法规未必与历史上对持枪权的理解相一致。

当国会于1968年颁布《枪支管制法》时，立法者理所当然地认为，大麻、镇静剂、兴奋剂或毒品的“非法使用者”不应被允许拥有枪支。他们在1986年重申了这一判断，将措辞改为涵盖任何“受管制物质”的非法使用者。

尽管后者法律被称为《枪支所有者保护法》，但它显然没有保护那些喜欢政治上不受待见的兴奋剂的枪支所有者，甚至没有保护敢为朋友或亲属使用处方药物的枪支所有者。它将所有这些人视为重罪犯。

最高法院在6月18日一致裁定，除非有证据表明特定吸毒者的枪支持有会对他自己或他人构成危险，否则这项政策是违宪的。它驳回了特朗普政府起诉一名拥有手枪的德克萨斯州大麻消费者的企图，称政府不能仅仅因为人们是大麻使用者就剥夺他们的第二修正案权利。

大法官们根据“我国枪支管制的历史传统”——这是法院第二修正案法学的北极星——得出了这一结论。一周后应用同样的标准，六名大法官表示，夏威夷通过立法禁止持证携带者未经业主明确许可将枪支带入私人企业，违反了第二修正案。

正如夏威夷所主张的，这种推定性枪支禁令只是为了保护既有的财产权。但正如大法官塞缪尔·阿利托在多数意见中所指出的，夏威夷的法律“与关于向公众开放的私有财产准入的普通法标准规则截然不同。”

根据这一规则，“每个人，包括合法携带枪支的人，都可以进入，除非被明确禁止进入，”阿利托写道。“相比之下，根据新的夏威夷法律，任何携带枪支的人都不得在未经财产所有者明确授权的情况下进入。”

阿利托指出，这一转变对持证携带者的“日常活动”施加了“严重限制”。这种效果并非偶然，因为夏威夷正试图规避最高法院2022年承认的携带手枪在公共场合自卫的宪法权利。

推翻夏威夷法律五天后，法院同意审理两起涉及禁止被政客刻意称为“突击武器”的广泛持有步枪的案件。自1989年加利福尼亚州开始，12个州颁布了此类法律，其核心是任意禁止折叠枪托、手枪式握把和枪管护套等功能。

这些法律针对的步枪很少被罪犯使用，但守法美国人经常使用，他们拥有超过3000万支此类步枪。后一点在宪法上具有相关性，因为最高法院曾表示，第二修正案适用于“普遍用于合法目的（如自卫）的‘可携带武器’”。

这些案件引发的长期酝酿的问题是，第二修正案是否保证“拥有AR-15平台和类似半自动步枪的权利”。如果是，那么对美国人被允许购买的武器的其他限制，如弹匣限制和加利福尼亚州的手枪规格要求，可能会面临宪法挑战。

已作出的判决也具有潜在的广泛影响。如果吸毒本身不能作为解除某人武装的理由，那么非暴力重罪定罪呢？如果夏威夷对公共持枪的广泛限制是违宪的，那么加利福尼亚州和纽约等州实施的范围广泛、地点特定的禁令同样在法律上似乎值得质疑。

在承认宪法持枪权近二十年后，最高法院开始解决关于其边界的遗留问题。热衷于管控的政客们可能不会喜欢这些答案。
articles.summary:
Over the course of 12 days last month, the Supreme Court upheld the gun rights of cannabis consumers, rejected Hawaii's default rule against firearms on private property open to the public, and agreed to address the constitutionality of "assault weapon" bans. That flurry of Second Amendment activity underlines the point that supposedly sensible gun regulations are not necessarily consistent with the right to arms as it was historically understood.

When Congress enacted the Gun Control Act in 1968, legislators took it for granted that an "unlawful user" of marijuana, depressants, stimulants, or narcotics should not be allowed to own a gun. They reaffirmed that judgment in 1986, changing the wording to encompass unlawful users of "any controlled substance."

Although the latter law was dubbed the Firearms Owners' Protection Act, it obviously did not protect gun owners with a taste for politically disfavored intoxicants, or even gun owners who dared to use medications prescribed for friends or relatives. It treated all those people as felons.

That policy, the Supreme Court unanimously ruled on June 18, is unconstitutional unless there is evidence that a particular drug user's gun possession would pose a danger to himself or others. It rejected the Trump administration's attempt to prosecute a Texas cannabis consumer who owned a pistol, saying the government may not strip people of their Second Amendment rights simply because they are marijuana users.

The justices reached that conclusion based on "this Nation's historical tradition of firearm regulation"—the lodestar of the Court's Second Amendment jurisprudence. Applying the same test a week later, six justices said Hawaii had violated the Second Amendment by making it illegal for carry-permit holders to bring guns into a private business without the owner's explicit permission.

As Hawaii presented it, that presumptive gun ban merely aimed to protect preexisting property rights. But as Justice Samuel Alito noted in the majority opinion, Hawaii's law "departs sharply from the standard common-law rule on access to private property held open to the public."

Under that rule, "everyone, including those lawfully carrying firearms, may enter unless expressly prohibited from doing so," Alito wrote. "By contrast, under the new Hawaii law, no one carrying a firearm may enter without the property owner's express authorization."

That switch, Alito noted, imposed "severe restrictions on the daily activities" of residents with carry permits. Nor was that effect incidental, since Hawaii was attempting an end run around the Supreme Court's 2022 decision recognizing a constitutional right to carry handguns in public for self-defense.

Five days after it overturned Hawaii's law, the Court agreed to hear a pair of cases involving bans on widely owned rifles that politicians tendentiously describe as "assault weapons." A dozen states, beginning with California in 1989, have enacted such laws, which hinge on arbitrarily prohibited features such as folding stocks, pistol grips, and barrel shrouds.

The rifles targeted by these laws are rarely used by criminals but commonly used by law-abiding Americans, who own more than 30 million of them. The latter point is constitutionally relevant because the Supreme Court has said the Second Amendment applies to "bearable arms" that are "in common use" for "lawful purposes like self-defense."

The long-simmering question posed by these cases is whether the Second Amendment guarantees "the right to possess AR-15 platform and similar semiautomatic rifles." If so, other restrictions on the arms Americans are allowed to buy, such as magazine limits and California's handgun specifications, may be vulnerable to constitutional challenges.

The decided cases also have potentially broad implications. If drug use, by itself, does not justify disarming someone, what about a nonviolent felony conviction? And if Hawaii's broad restriction on public gun possession was unconstitutional, the far-reaching, location-specific bans imposed by states such as California and New York likewise seem legally dubious.

Nearly two decades after recognizing a constitutional right to arms, the Supreme Court is beginning to address lingering questions about its contours. Control-happy politicians probably will not like the answers.

© Copyright 2026 by Creators Syndicate Inc.

The post Supreme Court Begins Answering Lingering Questions About Constitutional Constraints on Gun Control appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: $150K Settlement as to Community College "Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility" Requirements for Teaching
articles.content:
自由言论研究所代表贝克斯菲尔德学院历史学教授戴蒙·约翰逊发布报告称，约翰逊已达成一项和解协议，使其第一修正案保护成为永久性保护，并包括15万美元的律师费赔偿。

克恩社区学院区已同意和解约翰逊诉弗利格案，该联邦诉讼由自由言论研究所代表约翰逊教授提起。根据和解协议，联邦法院于2026年2月发布的临时禁令将转换为为期五年的永久禁令，禁止官员基于约翰逊在课堂上的言论、学术研究或作为普通公民的言论对其进行调查、纪律处分或解雇。

具体而言，该案质疑了加州法规中要求社区学院教职员工“采用反映DEIA和反种族主义原则的教学、学习和专业实践”，并要求证明“具备在加州社区学院教学、工作或领导方面与DEIA相关的能力”的规定。约翰逊教授是持不同意见的教职员工组织“自由派叛逃者研究所”（RIFL）的成员，他拒绝支持政府强制要求的观点，因此面临被解雇的威胁。

以下是我在2月份发布的约翰逊诉弗利格案判决书节选，由柯克·谢里夫法官（加州东区法院）撰写：

原告戴蒙·约翰逊是贝克斯菲尔德学院的历史学教授，他提起这项预防性执行挑战，寻求禁令和宣告性救济，以阻止贝克斯菲尔德学院和克恩社区学院区（KCCD）的官员对原告预期发表的言论执行《加州法规汇编》中的两项规定。这些规定要求约翰逊采用反映多元化、公平、包容和无障碍（DEIA）及反种族主义原则的“教学、学习和专业”实践，要求约翰逊“在加州社区学院教学、工作或领导方面建立与DEIA相关的能力”，并要求被告部分基于约翰逊在这些DEIA原则方面的能力来评估他。

约翰逊声称，DEIA法规强迫他发表言论，并因其观点而对他进行歧视，违反了第一修正案。他声称，他担心自己要么被强迫表达他不同意的观点，要么如果他继续拒绝表达被告期望的观点，或者如果他表达自己的相反观点，他就会受到惩罚。约翰逊可信地指出了他合理担心会被DEIA法规禁止的具体言论。正如第九巡回法院所认定的那样，他“根据其关于预期言论和拒绝支持[DEIA]原则的指控，已确立了'具体违反法律的计划'”。约翰逊诉弗利格案（第九巡回法院，2025年）（引用托马斯诉安克雷奇平等权利委员会案（第九巡回法院，2000年）（全院重审））。

约翰逊已确立，他预期发表的反映其反对DEIA法规和州DEIA政策的言论，涉及公共关注事项。只要DEIA法规禁止和压制约翰逊作为教授在其教学或学术研究中的预期言论，或他在校园内反对DEIA法规的“持不同意见的教职员工”团体中的工作，或他作为普通公民或从事课外言论的公共学者的身份，约翰逊对该预期言论拥有第一修正案权益。参见德默斯诉奥斯汀案（第九巡回法院，2014年）；雷吉斯诉考斯案（第九巡回法院，2025年）。被告未能证明“存在压制言论的合法行政利益，且该利益超过原告的第一修正案权利”。

约翰逊未能确立其对贝克斯菲尔德学院要求他完成强制性DEIA培训作为参与教职员工遴选委员会成员资格条件的诉求的胜诉可能性，因为政府可以通过此类员工培训表达其观点，且约翰逊未能证明该培训要求他个人认同政府观点为自己的观点。约翰逊也未能确立，作为贝克斯菲尔德学院平等机会与多样性顾问委员会（EODAC）成员的正式职务言论受保护，因为这是政府言论，而他未能证明该言论与学术研究或教学有充分联系。参见沙利文诉华盛顿大学案（第九巡回法院，2023年）（驳回了教职员工在州立大学的动物护理和使用委员会任职无权享有第一修正案保护的论点，因为他们“并非因此从事'教学和学术写作'”）。约翰逊还未能确立对DEIA法规进行面部挑战的胜诉可能性，因为DEIA法规适用于一系列非言论行为，且约翰逊未能证明这些法规相对于其明显的合法范围禁止了大量受保护的言论。

在考虑政府雇员的言论时，法院平衡政府“在促进其通过雇员执行的公共服务效率方面的利益”与雇员“在评论公共关注事项方面的利益”。皮克林诉教育委员会案（1968年）。在加西蒂奥诉塞瓦洛斯案（2006年）中，最高法院确立了皮克林例外，裁定“当公共雇员根据其正式职责发表声明时，雇员并非作为公民为第一修正案目的而发言，宪法不保护其通讯免受雇主纪律处分”。法院指出，“与学术奖学金或课堂指导相关的表达”可能涉及额外的宪法利益，但法院未决定加西蒂奥例外是否“以相同方式适用于涉及与奖学金或教学相关的言论的案件”。

第九巡回法院在德默斯案中解决了这个悬而未决的问题，结论是“加西蒂奥不能——确实，与第一修正案一致，也不能——适用于根据教师和教授的正式职责执行的与教学和学术写作相关的言论”。相反，“'与奖学金或教学相关的言论'受皮克林学说管辖，即使它是作为公共雇员正式职责的一部分发表的”。

约翰逊未能确立阻止被告要求他完成贝克斯菲尔德学院的DEIA培训才有资格在委员会任职的依据。约翰逊表示，他“无法成功完成DEIA培训，因为他不同意该培训强制要求的意识形态”。因此他“避免申请在委员会任职或完成所需的DEI培训”。但要求约翰逊完成DEIA培训才有资格在委员会任职并不涉及他的第一修正案权利，因为政府可以通过此类员工培训表达其观点，且约翰逊未表明他被要求个人认同培训中表达的观点。……在没有任何此类强迫认同或观点歧视的情况下，没有依据阻止被告在约翰逊可以在委员会任职之前要求其完成此类培训。参见诺格伦诉明尼苏达州人类服务厅案（第八巡回法院，2024年）（驳回了公共雇员关于培训不要求对其内容作出肯定同意的强迫言论索赔）。但被告不得强迫约翰逊在培训中支持DEIA原则发表言论，也不得因约翰逊拒绝认同关于DEIA原则的特定观点而对其进行制裁。

关于约翰逊代表RIFL的预期言论，在该动议的听证会上，被告辩称约翰逊作为RIFL教职员工负责人的角色是其正式职责的一部分，因此他的言论根据加西蒂奥属于“政府言论”。这一论点未在被告的简状中得到解决，而且无论如何都缺乏说服力。RIFL是一个教职员工参与的课外组织。虽然它可能像其他校园课外组织一样获得贝克斯菲尔德学院的认可，但没有证据表明RIFL代表学院或KCCD行政部门的发言。事实上，起诉状指控：“RIFL代表了校园中的少数立场。其成员的理念和理想与许多教职员工和学校行政管理部门所宣扬的观点总体上相反……”而且教职员工的“在公共关注事项上的异议言论根据加西蒂奥不构成政府言论”。

除DEIA培训要求（及相关的教职员工遴选委员会任职）以及作为EODAC成员的正式职务言论外，约翰逊已表明他的预期言论将以其教授身份发表，涉及与学术研究或教学相关的事项，或者是他在“工作时间外”作为普通公民的身份（包括作为公共学者）。因此，它受皮克林平衡法调整。

【适用皮克林平衡法，法庭】考虑被告是否表明“存在压制言论的合法行政利益，且该利益超过原告的第一修正案权利”。被告还必须表明DEIA法规将“以直接和实质的方式”缓解“真实的，而非仅仅是推测的”损害。被告未说明约翰逊的预期言论将如何对贝克斯菲尔德学院造成具体损害，也未说明DEIA法规如何缓解该损害。相反，他们只是声称了推进州“合法且实质的利益，即高效执行其教育使命、确保教学卓越以及为学生争取平等教育机会”的笼统利益。被告还声称了总体上能够“审查教职员工学术研究和教学的内容”、“偏爱特定的学术兴趣和方法”以及为“学生教学的质量和方法的学术标准”的笼统利益。

公立大学在对其课程行使一定控制权以及确保教职员工遵守学术和教学标准方面拥有合法利益。但虽然第一修正案不会保护未能满足最低学术能力水平的教职员工，或者，例如，坚持向被动接受的学生听众长时间讲授其个人政治观点而非教授指定的数学课程的教职员工，被告未指出约翰逊任何不符合此类学术能力或课程要求的预期言论。被告也未声称需要规范约翰逊的言论以防止歧视或骚扰，也未声称约翰逊打算从事根据皮克林测试应获得较低第一修正案保护的贬损性言论。参见汤普森诉中央谷学区第365号案（第九巡回法院，2025年）（认定涉及侮辱和暴力语言的言论根据皮克林测试只应获得“很小权重”）。

约翰逊不质疑贝克斯菲尔德学院总体上设定学术能力或课程要求的权威。相反，他寻求阻止被告强迫他认同他们对DEIA的观点，以及因他拒绝这样做或发表相反观点而对他进行制裁。“当一所大学希望其教授在公共关注事项上传达某一信息，而教授不希望向学生传达该信息时，这不是课堂管理问题，而是学术言论问题。”被告似乎也同意，他们不得根据DEIA法规因约翰逊的预期言论对其进行制裁，因为他们坚持认为“DEIA法规根据第53602(b)条和第53605(a)条的明文规定，不禁止任何言论，无论在课堂内外”。

被告只是阐述了对执行州DEIA法规的笼统兴趣，未指明对约翰逊预期言论的任何具体关切。这种笼统兴趣不能超过约翰逊不因其对DEIA事项的相反观点而受到制裁的第一修正案权利，也不能超过他不被迫为支持州关于DEIA事项的观点而发表言论的权利。

艾伦·古拉、考特尼·科贝洛和德尔·科尔德（自由言论研究所）担任约翰逊的代理律师。

这篇关于社区学院“多元化、公平、包容和无障碍”教学要求的15万美元和解案的文章首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
The Institute for Free Speech, which represented Bakersfield College Prof. Daymon Johnson, reports:

Johnson has secured a settlement that makes his First Amendment protections permanent—and includes a $150,000 payment for attorneys' fees.

Kern Community College District has agreed to settle Johnson v. Fliger, a federal lawsuit brought by the Institute for Free Speech on behalf of Professor Johnson. Under the settlement, the preliminary injunction that a federal court entered in February 2026 will be converted into a permanent injunction for a period of five years, barring officials from investigating, disciplining, or terminating Johnson based on his speech in the classroom, in his scholarship, or as a private citizen….

Specifically, the case challenged California regulations requiring community college faculty to "employ teaching, learning, and professional practices that reflect DEIA and anti-racist principles" and to demonstrate "proficiency in DEIA-related performance to teach, work, or lead within California community colleges." Professor Johnson, a member of the dissident faculty organization the Renegade Institute for Liberty (RIFL), refused to support the government-mandated views and faced the threat of termination as a result.

Here's the excerpt I posted in February from the Johnson v. Fliger decision by Judge Kirk Sherriff (E.D. Cal.):

Plaintiff Daymon Johnson, a professor of history at Bakersfield College, brings this pre-enforcement challenge seeking injunctive and declaratory relief to preclude officials of Bakersfield College and the Kern Community College District ("KCCD") from enforcing, as to his intended speech, two provisions of the California Code of Regulations. These provisions would require Johnson to employ "teaching, learning, and professional" practices reflecting diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility ("DEIA") and anti-racist principles, require Johnson to "establish proficiency in DEIA-related performance to teach, work, or lead within California community colleges," and require defendants to evaluate Johnson based in part on his proficiency in such DEIA principles….

Johnson alleges that the DEIA regulations compel his speech and discriminate against his viewpoint in violation of the First Amendment. He alleges that he fears either being compelled to express a viewpoint with which he disagrees or being punished if he continues to refuse to express defendants' desired viewpoint or if he expresses his contrary views. Johnson has credibly identified specific speech that he reasonably fears would be proscribed by the DEIA regulations. And as the Ninth Circuit has found, he "has established a 'concrete plan to violate the law' based on his allegations regarding his desired speech and his refusal to express support for [DEIA] principles." Johnson v. Fliger (9th Cir. 2025) (quoting Thomas v. Anchorage Equal Rts. Comm'n (9th Cir. 2000) (en banc)).

Johnson has established that his intended speech, which reflects his opposition to the DEIA regulations and to the state's DEIA policies, concerns a matter of public concern. To the extent the DEIA regulations proscribe and chill Johnson's intended speech in his teaching or scholarship as a professor, in his work with a "dissident faculty" group on campus that opposes the DEIA regulations, or in his capacity as a private citizen or public academic engaging in extracurricular speech, Johnson has a First Amendment interest in such intended speech. See Demers v. Austin (9th Cir. 2014); Reges v. Cauce (9th Cir. 2025). Defendants have failed to demonstrate "a legitimate administrative interest in suppressing the speech that outweigh[s] the plaintiff's First Amendment rights." …

Johnson has failed to establish a likelihood of success on his challenge to Bakersfield College's requirement that he complete mandatory DEIA training as a requirement to participate on faculty screening committees, as the government may express its views through such employee training, and Johnson fails to show that the training requires him to personally endorse the government's views as his own. Nor does Johnson establish that his official capacity speech as a member of Bakersfield College's Equal Opportunity & Diversity Advisory Committee ("EODAC") is protected speech, as it is government speech and he has not established that it is sufficiently connected to scholarship or teaching. See Sullivan v. Univ. of Washington (9th Cir. 2023) (rejecting argument that faculty members serving on state university's animal care and use committee were entitled to First Amendment protection, because they were "not thereby engaged in 'teaching and academic writing'"). Johnson also fails to establish a likelihood of success on his facial challenge to the DEIA regulations, as the DEIA regulations apply to a range of non-speech conduct and Johnson fails to show that they prohibit a substantial amount of protected speech relative to their plainly legitimate sweep….

In considering speech by government employees, courts balance the interests of the government "in promoting the efficiency of the public services it performs through its employees" and the employee's interest "in commenting upon matters of public concern." Pickering v. Board of Education (1968). In Garcetti v. Ceballos (2006), the Supreme Court established an exception to Pickering, holding that "when public employees make statements pursuant to their official duties, the employees are not speaking as citizens for First Amendment purposes, and the Constitution does not insulate their communications from employer discipline." The Court noted that "expression related to academic scholarship or classroom instruction" could implicate additional constitutional interests, but it did not decide whether the Garcetti exception would apply "in the same manner to a case involving speech related to scholarship or teaching."

The Ninth Circuit addressed that open question in Demers, concluding that "Garcetti does not—indeed, consistent with the First Amendment, cannot—apply to teaching and academic writing that are performed 'pursuant to the official duties' of a teacher and professor." Rather, "speech 'related to scholarship or teaching' is covered by the Pickering doctrine even if it was made pursuant to a public employee's official duties." …

Johnson fails to establish a basis to enjoin defendants from requiring that he complete Bakersfield College's DEIA training to be eligible to serve on a faculty screening committee. Johnson states that he "cannot successfully complete the DEIA training because [he] do[es] not agree with the ideology mandated by that training." He has therefore "refrain[ed] from applying to serve on a committee or doing the required DEI training." But the requirement that Johnson complete DEIA training to be eligible to serve on a committee does not implicate his First Amendment rights, as the government may express its views through such employee training, and Johnson does not show that he is required to personally endorse the views expressed in the training…. In the absence of any such compelled endorsement or viewpoint discrimination, there is no basis to enjoin defendants from requiring such training before Johnson may serve on a committee. See Norgren v. Minn. Dep't of Hum. Servs (8th Cir. 2024) (dismissing public employee's compelled speech claim where trainings did not require affirmative agreement with their content). But defendants may not compel Johnson to speak in support of DEIA principles as part of the training, or sanction Johnson for refusing to endorse a specific viewpoint on DEIA principles….

With respect to Johnson's intended speech on behalf of RIFL [Renegade Institute for Liberty, a faculty extracurricular group recognized by the College], at the hearing on this motion defendants argued that Johnson's role as faculty lead of RIFL was part of his official duties and that his speech would therefore be "government speech" under Garcetti. This argument was not addressed in defendants' briefs, and, in any event, is unpersuasive. RIFL is an extracurricular organization in which certain faculty members participate. While it may be recognized by Bakersfield College like other campus extracurricular organizations, there is no evidence that RIFL speaks as the College or KCCD administration. Indeed, the FAC alleges: "RIFL represents a minority position on campus. Its members' outlook and ideals stand in general opposition to those espoused by many faculty members and members of the school administration …." And faculty members' "dissenting speech on a matter of public concern is not government speech under Garcetti."

Except as to the DEIA training requirement (and related service on a faculty screening committee) or as to official speech as a member of the EODAC, Johnson has shown that his intended speech would be in his capacity as a professor and would concern matters related to scholarship or teaching, or that it would be in his off duty" capacity as a private citizen (including as a public academic). It is therefore subject to Pickering … balancing….

[Applying Pickering, the court] considers whether defendants have shown "a legitimate administrative interest in suppressing the speech that outweighs the plaintiff's First Amendment rights." Defendants must also show that the DEIA regulations would alleviate "real, not merely conjectural," harms in a "direct and material way." Defendants do not address how Johnson's desired speech would cause specific harm to Bakersfield College, nor how the DEIA regulations would alleviate that harm. Rather, they assert merely a generalized interest in advancing the State's "legitimate and substantial interest in efficiently carrying out its educational mission, ensuring teaching excellence, and in securing equal education opportunity for students." Defendants also assert a general interest in being able to "review the content of faculty scholarship and teaching," "favor particular scholarship interests and approaches," and establish "academic standards for the quality and method of delivering instruction to students."

A public university has a legitimate interest in exercising certain control over its curriculum and in ensuring faculty members' compliance with academic and teaching standards. But while the First Amendment would not protect a faculty member who fails to satisfy a minimum level of academic competence, or, for example, who insists on lecturing a captive student audience at length on his personal political views rather than teaching his assigned math course, defendants do not point to any intended speech by Johnson that would fail to meet such academic competence or course requirements. Nor do defendants allege that regulation of Johnson's speech is required to prevent discrimination or harassment, or that Johnson intends to engage in derogatory speech entitled to lesser First Amendment protection under the Pickering test. See Thompson v. Central Valley Sch. Dist. No. 365 (9th Cir. 2025) (finding that speech involving slurs and violent language was entitled to "little weight" under Pickering …).

Johnson does not challenge Bakersfield College's authority to set academic competence or curriculum requirements generally. Rather, he seeks to preclude defendants from compelling him to endorse their views regarding DEIA and from sanctioning him for refusing to do so or for stating his contrary views. "[W]hen a university wants its professors to communicate a message on a matter of public concern and a professor does not want to communicate that message to his students, that is not a matter of classroom management but one of academic speech." And defendants appear to agree that they may not sanction Johnson under the DEIA regulations for his intended speech, as they maintain that "[t]he DEIA regulations do not prohibit any speech by the plain text of sections 53602(b) and 53605(a), either in and out of the classroom."

Defendants have articulated only a general interest in enforcing the State's DEIA regulations, without identifying any specific concerns with Johnson's intended speech. That general interest does not outweigh Johnson's First Amendment right not to be sanctioned for having a contrary viewpoint on DEIA matters or his right not to be compelled to speak in support of the State's views on DEIA matters….

Alan Gura, Courtney Corbello, and Del Kolde (Institute for Free Speech) represent Johnson.

The post $150K Settlement as to Community College "Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility" Requirements for Teaching appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Eleventh Circuit Panel Strikes Down (by 2-1 Vote) Florida University Professor Speech Restrictions
articles.content:
以下是今日在佩内尔诉佛罗里达州州立大学理事会案中布里特·格兰特法官发表的冗长意见节选，查尔斯·威尔逊法官附议。请注意，关于此案的两项主要先例是毕晓普诉阿罗诺夫案，两份意见都对其进行了详细讨论：

[A.] 本案涉及的条款禁止佛罗里达州公立学院和大学的学生接受任何"宣扬、促进、推进、灌输或强制"八项与种族和性别相关概念的训练或教学：

- 某一种族、肤色、国籍或性别的人在道德上优于另一种族、肤色、国籍或性别的人。
- 一个人因其种族、肤色、国籍或性别而固有地是种族主义者、性别歧视者或压迫者，无论是有意识还是无意识的。
- 个人的道德品格或作为享有特权或受压迫者的身份必然由其种族、肤色、国籍或性别决定。
- 某一种族、肤色、国籍或性别的人不能且不应该在不考虑种族、肤色、国籍或性别的情况下对待他人。
- 一个人因其种族、肤色、国籍或性别而对同一种族、肤色、国籍或性别的其他成员过去的行为承担责任，或应因此受到歧视或遭受不利待遇。
- 一个人因其种族、肤色、国籍或性别而应受到歧视或遭受不利待遇，以实现多元化、公平或包容。
- 一个人因其种族、肤色、国籍或性别而对同一种族、肤色、国籍或性别的其他成员过去参与的行为（该人未参与）承担个人责任，并因这些行为感到内疚、痛苦或其他形式的心理困扰。
-  Merit、卓越、勤奋、公平、中立、客观和种族色盲等美德是种族主义或性别歧视的，或者是由某一特定种族、肤色、国籍或性别的人创造出来压迫另一种族、肤色、国籍或性别的人的。

虽然禁止"宣扬"任何这些概念，但批评它们并不受限。此外，该法案允许讲师以中立方式介绍这些观点，允许"作为更大课程的一部分"进行讨论——只要"以客观的方式进行教学，不认可这些概念"。该法律未说明其限制延伸至课堂之外多远——校园外的演讲和其他场合可能涉及其中…… {该法案的条款适用于所有公立学校，从幼儿园到研究生院，但原告仅挑战其在高等教育机构的适用。}

[B.] [被告辩称]由于佛罗里达州"拨款资助"其大学的公立教育，它有权"说它想说的"，依据政府言论原则。这是 一个宽泛的主张，且其引用的政府言论先例并不支持这一主张。这些案件涉及公共纪念碑、汽车牌照等——与学术教学毫不相关。这些判决的要旨是：当政府言论时，它可以 选择其信息。正如最高法院所言，没有必要要求一个支持回收利用的地方政府包含当地垃圾公司的反驳意见。佛罗里达州所依赖的经典政府言论案件也是关于政府本身表达某些观点——而不是禁止员工认可其他观点。即便如此，该州声称："如果提议汽车牌照口号的私人言论是政府言论，那么州立大学受雇教育者的课堂言论肯定也是。"但为什么？佛罗里达州没有说明…… {当然，大学对其自身课程有控制权……它们无需提供例如学生想上的每一门课。但这并不能解决，甚至没有真正涉及本案中的问题。}

在考虑政府是否在言论时，有多种因素在起作用。若干考虑可能相关，包括"政府是否保持对言论的控制"、"该类型言论传统上是否传达政府信息"以及"公众是否合理地认为政府认可了该言论"。

我们首先考虑政府是否保持对这里言论的控制。该州传统上对高等教育的控制相对较少，尤其是与公立中小学相比。佛罗里达州通过法规施加的课程要求很少。即使有，也涉及基本通识教育要求——比如学生毕业必须修多少核心课程以及传播学课程必须达到什么标准。（这样的课程"必须使学生能够有效沟通"）。

而佛罗里达州高等教育体系的"目的和使命"，根据州法律，是"使所有学生能够参与知识探索和个人发展"，"发现和传播知识"，"促进教育机会的多样性"，并"培养学生对知识和人性的更高敏感性"。这些广泛的目标与对大学教授传达的信息进行无限制控制不相符。佛罗里达州大学的相对独立性与其对中小学的近乎全面权威不同，在中小学，"州学术标准确定了该州教授课程的核心内容"。在行使这一广泛权力时，州教育委员会通过了一项全面的教育标准清单——近7300项，涵盖每个学科和每个年级。此外，公立学校教师必须"使用要求的书籍和材料"，"遵循规定的课程"，仅"采用批准的教学方法"。总之，州政府传统上控制着中小学的课程，但在高等教育领域仅提供最低限度的法定指导。当我们将该州与政府言论领域中所见的国家控制水平进行比较时——几乎是完全控制——佛罗里达州对大学讲座的相对超脱更加说明问题。一些例子说明了这一点。在一个涉及州冠军赛期间使用体育场扩音器的案件中，本院解释播音员的话是"完全脚本化的"，"每一个字"都是由州政府雇员写的。在另一个涉及美国农业部支持的推广牛肉运动案件中，最高法院指出政府行使"对使用的每一个字的最终批准权"。佛罗里达州不能——也不可能——对每一个高等教育课堂讲座都施加这种控制。事实上，佛罗里达州并未主张它控制了教授所说的每一个字，只是主张它可以。常识在这里也发挥作用。真的有人认为每所公立大学的每位教授在每门课上都在传达该州政治分支的官方路线吗？因此，虽然政府言论原则"不需要全能"，但佛罗里达州远未表现出将教授讲座归类为政府言论所需的那种控制程度。

第二和第三个因素——该言论是否传统上传达政府信息，以及公众是否将该言论视为政府认可——在这里密切相关。当然，在某种程度上，公众认为该州对其大学的课程决定给予了认可。法院也承认大学至少对"课程的重点和一般主题"有控制权。

但本法的文本并非寻求发展某所大学、某个专业甚至某门课程的课程。相反，它试图在该州的每一所大学和任何课程中禁止一组主题上不受欢迎的言论。而且它以一种与佛罗里达州长期存在的（想必仍然有效的）管理大学的法规相矛盾的方式做到这一点。例如，佛罗里达州法律要求州立大学持续评估该校的"知识自由和观点多样性"以及"竞争性观点和视角的表达程度"。如果该言论受第一修正案保护，理事会不得限制学生"接触或观察他们可能觉得不舒服、不受欢迎、令人反感或冒犯的观点和意见"。这些原则与最高法院承认的"课堂特别是一个'思想市场'"相一致。它们与大学教授作为国家喉舌传达政府信息的观念不一致。这些法规（连同常识）明确表明，公众——甚至是该州——都认识到大学教授是独立发言的，而不是对每一个他们传达的观点都有政府认可…… [此外，最高法院关于政府雇员言论的领先案件]主要集中在纪律处分一个已经发言的单一雇员——"对一名雇员的言论及其对该雇员公共责任影响的事后分析"。合众国诉国家财政部雇员联盟案（1995年）。在这里，政府试图控制雇员言论的情况处于另一个极端。该法案是对该州每位公立大学教授言论的广泛的事先限制。因为该法案"在言论发生之前就对其产生了寒蝉效应"，该州"对这种表达限制的负担比对单一纪律处分更大"……

[C.] [然而]我们仍然面临一个困难的问题：个人自由法案的言论限制能否对这些教授合宪适用？毕竟，尽管该州提供直接控制很少，但佛罗里达州（与其他州一样）基本上负责其公立大学，任命理事会大多数成员，提供大量资金，并建立某些核心课程标准。"联邦法官不应该充当代理院长或教育者。"而且因为"各州在教育领域历来是主权者"，"教育政策的制定和执行的传统角色"属于它们——而不是我们。这使得联邦法院"不愿侵犯州和地方教育机构的特权"。但这些先例，以及它们在第一修正案目录中为学术自由保留空间的对应原则，未能为我们今天考虑的问题提供直接答案。我们……[转向]毕晓普诉阿罗诺夫案（第十一巡回法院，1991年），该案与我们一样问到："学校在触及教师的第一修正案权利之前，可以在多大程度上控制课堂指导。"

该案源于阿拉巴马大学禁止一名教授利用课堂时间描述其宗教观点。其中一些评论涉及他相信"人类生理学背后的创造性力量"。其他则是为了分享他的信仰，公开他的偏见，并为学生提供如何应对学业压力的建议。但学生们想要的是另一回事——一门不间断的运动生理学课程。在投诉足够多之后，大学指示毕晓普停止，禁止他"在教学时间内呈现他的宗教观点，即使它代表了他对学科主题的专业意见"。我们最终驳回了该教授对该限制的言论自由挑战。在这里，佛罗里达州认为大学的回应与佛罗里达州政治分支对所有公立学院和大学教授的预防性言论禁令的合宪性之间存在直接联系。事实并非如此。毕晓普案本身指示，存在"没有替代方案来进行逐案调查，以确定当局的合法利益是否明显足以限制教师的言论"……

[按照]毕晓普案的指引，我们承认大学系统的"基本教育使命"，该使命授权"对课堂内言论使用'合理限制'，而这种限制在该州不能在课堂外审查"。沿着同样的思路，我们承认黑泽尔伍德学区诉库迈尔案的观点（在毕晓普案中得到认可），即"教育工作者通过对他们合理相关的合法教育关切，对学校赞助的表达活动中学生或教授言论的风格和内容行使编辑控制，不构成对第一修正案的侵犯"。

这些因素归结为承认，在这里，就像在其他第一修正案案件中一样，我们被要求在教授的利益与该州的利益之间取得平衡。



然而，政府在这一方程式中的利益相当有限：编辑控制被视为理所当然，但限制必须合理且与正当的教育关切相关。在进行这一分析时，我们考虑毕晓普案的三个一般因素：言论的背景；国家的“公共雇主地位”；以及“对学术自由的强烈偏好，作为第一修正案言论权的附属”。

第一，背景。此处的背景与毕晓普案相同：“大学课堂在特定上课时间内”。毕晓普案还指出，“当学生投诉表明存在明显强制时——即使教授并非有意为之——大学的利益最为明显。”诚然，“强制”一词让人联想到 Establishment Clause 先例（尤其是在毕晓普案时期），但我们认为这一关切在此同样相关。佛罗里达州的限制是——正如该州承认的——试图通过削减大学中思想的自由交流来强制统一思想——而大学正是传统上被视为表达和真理探索的实验室。通过压制“个人思想和表达”来强迫接受某些信念，在课堂背景下“尤其”危险，“在那里，国家是在我们思想和实验传统的核心——我们的知识和哲学传统的背景下采取行动的。”与毕晓普案不同，在该案中大学是在推进反强制目标，佛罗里达州在此主张相反的利益——强制大学教师（进而延伸至学生）回避某一类观点。佛罗里达州关于这些观点有多危险的判断是否正确，与我们作为法院的调查无关。“无论这些观点多么有争议，允许政府设定辩论的条款都是毒药，而非解药。”

第二，我们考虑作为雇主的州的利益。“法院一致认为，学校管理层至少可以确定课程的侧重点和一般主题范围。”毫无疑问。“与课程管理权相关的是，对教师在课堂内外的行为也有一定管理权，只要该行为与课程显著相关或可能造成大学背书的表象。”我们认为，这一认识大大降低了在学术领域执行第一修正案会使大学或其他政府当局无力做出总体课程决定或排除明显偏离众所周知范围的言论——与主题无关的个人跑题、阴谋论等——的风险。我们早期的案例支持同样的观点。在一个案例中，我们认定一所大学解雇一名教授的言论“严重损害了其作为讲师的效力”并不构成宪法错误。弗格森诉托马斯案（第五巡回法院，1970年）。在另一个案例中，我们驳回了一位教授的第一修正案挑战，该教授因以下行为被拒绝授予终身教职：让学生用两门课程的学分换取实际上只修一门课程；给大多数学生高分而他们几乎没做什么作业；发表“不真实且具有误导性的公开声明”；表现出缺乏“专业精神和成熟”。梅吉尔诉校董会案（第五巡回法院，1976年）。在毕晓普案中，我们再次认定当大学认定一位教授的离题言论阻碍了他正确教授生理学的能力时，不构成第一修正案违反。这些案例表明，即使在保留学术自由空间的情况下，大学管理者（或许还有其他政府官员）也有合法利益确保教授的课堂言论既与课程“相关”又“具有专业能力”。

佛罗里达州的辩护理由与这些理论不符。佛罗里达州没有主张促进课堂效率或适当教育学生的利益，而是声称其“主权判断”是被禁止的观点“违背了州最珍贵的理想”。但推进珍贵的州理想与普通的工作场所管理关切相去甚远，更不用说正当的教育学利益了。更重要的是，即使这些观点中有许多是大错特错的，它们也并非完全无关紧要，至少在某些课程作业中是这样的。佛罗里达州自己似乎也承认了这一事实，允许讨论这些观点，同时禁止为其背书。该州确实为法律提出了另一个辩护理由——即“防止公共教育中可恶的种族歧视的利益”。但正如我们在考虑限制私营雇主言论的配套条款时已经裁定的，该法案并没有做到这一点。歧视“通常意味着区别对待”，而“该法案并不规范差别待遇”——它限制的是州政治当局认为令人反感的言论。禁止教授积极讨论某一观点并不等同于规范歧视，后者确实是州可以做的。一位教授宣扬某一观点与一位嘲笑学生、威胁学生或针对学生的教授并不处于同等地位。该法案不限制（也不声称限制）任何形式的实际歧视。它反而假定仅仅接触州视为错误观点本身就是一种严重伤害。但意见分歧不等于歧视……

第三，我们考虑毕晓普案中所说的“对学术自由的强烈偏好”。正如我们在那里（也在此）指出的，学术自由在第一修正案判例法中受到“大量”赞扬，“对宪法自由的警惕保护在美国的学校社区中比任何地方都更为重要。”事实上，“我们国家的未来取决于通过广泛的接触得到培养的领导者，这种培养是通过思想的大胆交流实现的，通过众多声音的碰撞发现真理，而非通过任何形式的权威选择。”

毕晓普案再次证明了这一点。诚然，该法院也指出，在其他背景下“关于学术自由的声明”“不能被推断为剥夺学校对其课程的控制权”。为此，我们与毕晓普案一起认可它承认“学术自由不仅在教师和学生之间独立、不受抑制的思想交流中蓬勃发展，而且在某种程度上不一致地，也在学院自身的自主决策中蓬勃发展。”相比之下，佛罗里达州的法案剥夺了学院对所禁止主题做出任何决策的权力。而且州的理论没有界限——它将允许立法机关以任何理由阻止任何主题的任何言论……限制越广泛，被限制言论的人越多，就必须接受越严格的审查。对单个问题教授的教学进行逐案限制是第一修正案确认大学课程权的主流做法。但佛罗里达州试图将这些先例拉伸以适应一个全新的背景：不是一所大学纪律处分一位教授的个别不当行为，而是一个州政府禁止所有教授分享政治上不受欢迎的观点。{毕晓普案没有就州控制课程的权力做出任何裁定；它保护的是大学的课程控制权。}……

[D.] 该法案对学术自由的影响因其缺乏明确界定而加剧……例如，当一位教授为课堂布置了一篇推广被禁概念之一的阅读材料时，该教授是否使学生“接受了宣传、促进、推进、灌输或强迫该学生……相信”该概念的“教学”？当一位教授邀请一位只呈现辩论一方的客座演讲者时又如何？{被告告诉我们，除非教授认可演讲者的言论，否则邀请推广八项概念之一演讲者的教授不会违反该法案。这不是解决问题而是加剧了问题，在模糊之上叠加模糊。}或者，一位使用苏格拉底方法教授被禁概念之一的教授呢？这些讨论会是“客观的”吗？各方在每个问题上都持不同意见。更不用说各概念定义中缺乏明确语言的问题了。仅举一个例子，“道德上优越”包括什么，它是相对于谁的视角来衡量的？……

以下是洛加亚法官冗长异议的一些节选：

[A.] 教授原告是州雇员，涉及的言论发生在他们州立大学的州立课程中，这些课程由在州公立大学注册的学生修读以获得学分。根据多数意见，只要限制“合理且与正当的教育关切相关”，州就可以限制教授的言论。多数意见还告诉我们，州可以合法禁止教授讨论“无关”材料、“专业上不称职”材料、“阴谋论及类似内容”。

多数意见保证这些是“众所周知界限”。但在实践中，这些“界限”确立了一种法官创设的测试，既不可行，其“对宪法的政策驱动方法”将第一修正案降格为无论州的课堂教学利益如何都反映司法偏好的工具……需要明确的是，第一修正案保护公共广场上的所有观点，无论它们是传统的还是争议的。但它并不强制所有观点都值得获得州立背书……IFA 涉及课堂教学，允许教授讨论歧视性概念但不为其背书或强迫学生接受……作为雇主的州的利益优先于教授教授个人观点的利益。[B.] 毕晓普案……应该终结此案……与毕晓普案一样，本案涉及对教授课堂言论的限制。毕晓普案已经裁定，关于课堂教学，州的利益优先于个别教授的利益……首先……[在毕晓普案中，我们注意到阿拉巴马大学合理担心该教授的言论会对学生产生“强制性影响”：学生会觉得为了获得好成绩必须表现出对该教授宗教观点的兴趣。多数意见说本案“不同于”毕晓普案，因为在后者中“大学是在推进反强制目标”。在这里，佛罗里达州据称“主张相反的利益——强制[其]大学教师”回避“一组特定观点”。

恭敬地说，这一区分站不住脚。毕晓普案处理的是针对学生的强制；我们没有对教授遭受的任何强制表示关切。换言之，毕晓普案中的教授与本案的教授原告一样，用多数意见的话说，是被“强制”“回避一组特定观点”。这就是毕晓普案提起诉讼的原因。参见毕晓普案，多数意见试图将佛罗里达州的利益描绘成与毕晓普案中的利益“相反”，这是说不通的。其次……[关于教授言论与主题的相关性问题，毕晓普案假设该教授的宗教观点影响了他关于运动生理学的专业观点。我们裁定大学可以阻止他“在教学时间内呈现其宗教观点，即使它代表了他关于其学科的专业意见。”同样，我们认识到该教授表达了“其工作中碰巧源于宗教来源的观点”。

然而，大学仍然可以得出结论——与第一修正案一致——该教授的“观点不应在他在大学所教的课程中被呈现。”毕晓普案的核心并非如多数意见所暗示的那样是宗教与科学无关，而是州作为公共雇主可以决定其课堂教什么。在专业能力问题上，我们明确拒绝“衡量”该教授的观点[在毕晓普案中]是否“站得住脚”。因此我们的决定与“专业[]能力”无关。无论如何，一个所谓“专业上不称职”言论的例外将允许多数意见其他地方所谴责的“对不受欢迎观点的赤裸裸禁止”。无论被贴上“不能胜任”还是“不合理”的标签，结果都是一样的：州可以决定其员工在课堂教学中可以认可哪些观点，哪些不可以。第三，毕晓普案考虑了第一修正案“对学术自由作为言论权附属的强烈偏好”……[但是]毕晓普案裁定，教授的“学术自由和言论自由利益”最终不能“在课堂内取代大学的利益”。我们注意到，当教授“作为独立教育者或研究者行事”时，州不能限制其言论，但当教授“作为课程讲师行事”时，州可以限制其言论。

[C.] 在我看来，唯一的合法区别在于毕晓普案涉及对一位教授言论的限制，而本案涉及由佛罗里达州立法机关颁布的普遍适用法规。



但这一差异不应改变本案的判决结果。首先，Bishop案并未将“合理性”视为在每个新案件中重新平衡利益的许可证。使这些限制变得“合理”的，是一个单一、具体的事实：“它们仅适用于课堂言论”。这一限制承载了整个分析。而当按照Bishop案的要求对IFA进行“狭义解释”时，因为其限制涉及第一修正案自由，其适用范围同样限于课堂授课。此外，该法案明确允许“对所列概念进行讨论”，条件是“任何培训或教学均以客观方式进行，且不认可这些概念”。它并未“禁止”或“拒绝”任何观点。IFA禁止佛罗里达州的教师强迫学生相信他们必须接受某种规定的观点（例如可能为了获得好成绩或在课堂上获得优待）。因此，Bishop案所达成的平衡同样解决了本案。……多数意见援引美国诉国家财政部雇员联合会案（1995年），提出“对大量雇员的言论施加广泛的预防性限制，需要更高的标准来维护其权益”……毫无疑问，与Bishop案不同，IFA适用于佛罗里达州所有公立大学教授的课堂授课。但为什么这会造成差异？NTEU案涉及与公共雇员工作职责无关的言论。相比之下，本案涉及佛罗里达州教授在履行教学职责时的言论。而Bishop案明确裁定，当个别教授与其雇主在“他所教课程的内容问题上”产生分歧时，雇主“在此类争议中必须拥有最终决定权”。没有多少理由认为这种平衡在大规模适用时会发生变化。[D.]无论如何，本案中南棕榈滩州有着重大利益。IFA禁止教导人们应该根据肤色来判断他人，禁止教导人们“不应尝试”平等对待其他种族，以及禁止教导其他种族“应该受到歧视”。……[A]教授对特定观点的认同具有的分量可能会扭曲真正的学术交流。正如Bishop案所解释的，教授的话语并非在真空中运作；它们具有“强迫效应”，因为教授对学生成绩拥有决定权。设想一位教授说哈马斯10月7日对以色列的袭击是“令人振奋的”，是值得庆祝和鼓励的。如果教授将此作为课堂授课的一部分，那么犹太学生会觉得可以自由地戴着圆顶小帽在课堂上发表相反观点、在评分作业中表达异议，或在考试中作答吗？值得怀疑。多数意见保证“提倡特定观点的教授与嘲笑学生、威胁学生或针对学生的教授并不处于同一地位”，但这无助于解释上述现实。佛罗里达州在其自身课堂内遏制此类教授认可的行为，完全在其权力范围之内。……[E.]多数意见对本反对意见的大部分批评归结为所谓“国家控制课程权力”与“大学控制课程权力”之间的区别。

但佛罗里达州法律使得多数意见的大学—州区别毫无立足之地。佛罗里达州宪法规定：“应建立单一的州立大学系统”，“每个公立大学应由一个理事会管理”，“一个董事会应治理州立大学系统”。佛罗里达州将教授原告所在的各大学列为“州立大学”。而且佛罗里达州声明，州立大学的“理事会是州政府行政部门的组成部分”。实际上，在第十一修正案背景下，佛罗里达州对其公共教育系统的控制如此紧密，以至于其理事会被视为“州的臂膀”……

综合来看，这些权威依据清楚地表明，佛罗里达州的公立大学在州的控制和管理下进行言论和行为。事实上，IFA作为州对其自身雇员的指令，规定了课堂授课时应遵循的标准，用佛罗里达州的话说，这些教师是在“教州的课程，在州的教室里，在州的时间里，拿着州的工资”。正如本院在Bishop案中承认的那样，公共雇主有权“合理地控制其课程内容，特别是课堂时间传授的内容”。

{最高法院区分了州“试图指导私立大学的言论内容”，在此“会产生明显的第一修正案问题”，与州“试图指导公共教育机构的言论内容”，在此“会产生复杂的第一修正案问题，因为政府同时既是言论者又是监管者”。宾夕法尼亚大学诉平等就业机会委员会案（1990年）。}……

[F.]多数意见的规则旨在避免其认为的州不当的观点歧视，但这样做反而支持了自身形式的观点歧视。当然，一定程度的观点歧视对于确保任何学术机构的有效运作都是必要的。多数意见承认这一点，保留了模糊的例外条款——比如“合理性”——来区分第一修正案下允许和不允许的禁止。它强调边界线是“众所周知的”。它保证“阴谋论”和“类似内容”将不被容忍。但这些例外并未消除观点歧视。{尽管它保证“不愿对[这些]观点的价值做出任何判断”，但多数意见恰恰做了这样的判断。根据其自身说法，它认为“大学或其他政府当局”可能“排除超出众所周知的范围的言论”。但是，“超出众所周知的范围”是什么意思，为什么必须由这个多数意见来决定？这些问题没有得到回答。相反，多数意见同时将某些类别的言论标记为可以适当排除的，同时坚持“我们的职责不是”评判有争议观点的价值。}

换言之，多数意见认识到，必须有人来确定公立大学可以教授什么的界限。通过其例外条款，多数意见将这一决定权从人民手中转移到了联邦法官手中。现在的标准变成了：主审法官是否认为教授的观点在可允许的范围内？……多数意见的规则仍然允许“对不受欢迎言论的赤裸裸禁止”，但授权在司法机构创建、解释和执行的例外条款下进行观点歧视。请注意，我与我的学生代表学术自由联盟提交了一份法庭之友简报，支持对该法律的挑战。第十一巡回上诉法院小组以2比1票数推翻佛罗里达州大学教师言论限制的消息首次出现在Reason.com上。
articles.summary:
Some excerpts from today's very long opinion in Pernell v. Fla. Bd. of Governors of State Univ. by Judge Britt Grant, joined by Judge Charles Wilson; note that the leading Eleventh Circuit precedent on this is Bishop v. Aronov, which both opinions discuss in considerable detail:

[A.] The provision at issue here disallows any "training or instruction that espouses, promotes, advances, inculcates, or compels" students at Florida's public colleges and universities to believe any of eight concepts relating to topics like race and sex:

  1. Members of one race, color, national origin, or sex are morally superior to members of another race, color, national origin, or sex.
  2. A person, by virtue of his or her race, color, national origin, or sex, is inherently racist, sexist, or oppressive, whether consciously or unconsciously.
  3. A person's moral character or status as either privileged or oppressed is necessarily determined by his or her race, color, national origin, or sex.
  4. Members of one race, color, national origin, or sex cannot and should not attempt to treat others without respect to race, color, national origin, or sex.
  5. A person, by virtue of his or her race, color, national origin, or sex, bears responsibility for, or should be discriminated against or receive adverse treatment because of, actions committed in the past by other members of the same race, color, national origin, or sex.
  6. A person, by virtue of his or her race, color, national origin, or sex, should be discriminated against or receive adverse treatment to achieve diversity, equity, or inclusion.
  7. A person, by virtue of his or her race, color, sex, or national origin, bears personal responsibility for and must feel guilt, anguish, or other forms of psychological distress because of actions, in which the person played no part, committed in the past by other members of the same race, color, national origin, or sex.
  8. Such virtues as merit, excellence, hard work, fairness, neutrality, objectivity, and racial colorblindness are racist or sexist, or were created by members of a particular race, color, national origin, or sex to oppress members of another race, color, national origin, or sex.

Though "promot[ing]" any of these concepts is barred, criticizing them is not. Beyond that, the Act allows instructors to introduce the ideas in a neutral fashion, permitting discussion "as part of a larger course"—so long as "instruction is given in an objective manner without endorsement of the concepts." And the law does not say how far past the classroom its restrictions extend—off-campus speeches and other settings may be in play….

{The Act's terms apply to all public schools, starting with kindergarten and going through graduate school, but the plaintiffs challenge only its application to postsecondary schools.}

[B.] [The defendants argue] that because Florida "appropriates public funds" to support public education at the State's universities, it is "entitled to say what it wishes" at those universities [under the government speech doctrine]. That is a sweeping assertion, and one that is unsupported by the cited government speech precedents.

Those cases are about public monuments, license plates, and the like—not anything close to academic teaching. The thrust of their holdings is that when the government speaks it can choose its message. There is no need, as the Supreme Court put it, for a local government that supports recycling to include a counterargument from the local trash company. The classic government speech cases Florida leans on are also about the government itself expressing certain ideas—not banning employees from endorsing others. Even so, the State asserts that "if the speech of private individuals proposing license plate slogans is government speech, then surely the classroom speech of state-employed educators at state universities is too." But why? Florida does not say….

{Of course[,] universities have control over their own curriculums…. They need not offer, for instance, every class a student would like to take. But that does not resolve, or really even address, the issues in this case.}

A variety of factors are at play when considering whether the government is speaking …. Several considerations may be relevant, including "whether the government maintains control over the speech," "whether the type of speech has traditionally communicated government messages," and "whether the public would reasonably believe that the government has endorsed the speech."

We first consider whether the government maintains control over the speech here. The State has traditionally exercised relatively little control over postsecondary education, especially as compared to public primary or secondary education. Florida imposes few curricular requirements by statute. And those that it does impose involve basic general education requirements—things like how many core classes students must take to graduate and what standards a communications course must meet. (Such a course "must afford students the ability to communicate effectively.")

And the "purpose and mission" of Florida's postsecondary educational system, as defined by state law, is to "enable[] all students to participate in the search for knowledge and individual development," to "discover and disseminate knowledge," to "foster[] diversity of educational opportunity," and to "develop in students heightened intellectual, cultural, and humane sensitivities." These broad ambitions are inconsistent with unrestricted control over the messages conveyed by university professors.

The relative independence of Florida's universities diverges from the State's near plenary authority over primary and secondary education, where "state academic standards establish the core content of the curricula to be taught in the state." In exercising this broad authority, the State Board of Education has adopted a comprehensive list of educational standards—nearing 7,300 in number and spanning every subject and every grade. To meet these standards, moreover, public school teachers must "us[e] the books and materials required," "follow[] the prescribed courses of study," and only "employ[] approved methods of instruction." The state government, in short, has traditionally controlled the curriculum in primary and secondary schools, but offers only minimal statutory guidance in the postsecondary education context.

The State's relative detachment from college lectures is even more revealing when compared to the level of state control we see in the government speech context—nearly total. A few examples illustrate the point. In one case about the use of a stadium loudspeaker during a state championship game, this Court explained that the announcer's remarks were "entirely scripted," with "[e]very word" written by a state employee. And in another involving a USDA-sponsored pro-beef campaign, the Supreme Court noted that the government exercised "final approval authority over every word used." Florida does not—and could not—exercise that kind of control over every postsecondary class lecture. Indeed, Florida does not argue that it has controlled every word that university professors say, only that it can.

And common sense also has a role to play here. Does anyone really think that every professor in every class at every public university is putting forward the official line of the State's political branches? So while the government speech doctrine "does not require omnipotence," Florida has not come close to exhibiting the degree of control necessary to classify professors' lectures as government speech.

The second and third factors—whether the speech has traditionally communicated government messages and whether the public would view the speech as endorsed by the government—are closely related here. Certainly at some level the public perceives a seal of approval from the State for the curricular decisions of its universities. And courts have recognized that the universities themselves at least have control over "the parameters of focus and general subject matter of curriculum."

But the text of this law does not seek to develop the curriculum of a university, of a major, or even of a class. Instead, it seeks to bar disfavored speech on one set of topics at every university and in any class. And it does so in a way that contradicts Florida's longstanding (and presumably still valid) statutes governing its universities.

Florida law, for instance, requires state universities to continually assess "the intellectual freedom and viewpoint diversity" and "the extent to which competing ideas and perspectives are presented" at the school. And the Board of Governors is forbidden from limiting students' "access to, or observation of, ideas and opinions that they may find uncomfortable, unwelcome, disagreeable, or offensive," if that speech is protected by the First Amendment.

These principles are consistent with the Supreme Court's recognition that the "classroom is peculiarly the 'marketplace of ideas.'" And they are inconsistent with the idea that university professors communicate government messages as mouthpieces of the state. These statutes (together with common sense) make clear that the public—and even the State—both recognize that university professors speak independently and without government endorsement of every idea they communicate….

[Moreover, the Supreme Court's leading government employee speech cases] centered on disciplining a single employee who had already spoken—"a post hoc analysis of one employee's speech and its impact on that employee's public responsibilities." United States v. Nat'l Treasury Emps. Union (1995). Here, the government's attempt to control employee speech is at the other end of the spectrum. The Act is a wide-ranging, ex ante restriction on the speech of every public university professor in the state. Because the Act "chills potential speech before it happens," the State's "burden is greater with respect to this statutory restriction on expression than with respect to an isolated disciplinary action." …

[C.] [W]e are still left with a difficult question: can the Individual Freedom Act's speech restrictions be constitutionally applied to these professors? After all, though it offers little direct control, Florida (like other states) is fundamentally in charge of its public universities, appointing most members of the Board of Governors, providing considerable funding, and establishing certain core curricular standards.

"Federal judges should not be ersatz deans or educators." And because "States historically have been sovereign" in the field of education, the "traditional role in the formulation and execution of educational policy" belongs to them—not us. That leaves federal courts "reluctan[t] to trench on the prerogatives of state and local educational institutions." But these precedents, like their counterparts reserving space in the First Amendment catalogue for academic freedom, fail to provide a direct answer to the question we consider today.

We … [turn] to Bishop v. Aronov (11th Cir. 1991), which asked as we do here, "to what degree a school may control classroom instruction before touching the First Amendment rights of a teacher." … [T]hat case arose when the University of Alabama barred one of its professors from using class time to describe his religious views. Some of his comments concerned his belief in "the creative force behind human physiology." Others were made to share his faith, be open about his biases, and offer suggestions for students on how to deal with academic stress. But the students sought something else—an uninterrupted course on exercise physiology. After enough complaints, the University directed Bishop to stop, forbidding him from "presenting his religious viewpoint during instructional time, even to the extent that it represent[ed] his professional opinion about his subject matter." We ultimately rejected the professor's free speech challenge to that restriction.

Here, Florida suggests that a direct line connects the constitutionality of the University's response to Bishop with the constitutionality of the Florida political branches' prophylactic speech ban on all public college and university professors. Not so. Bishop itself instructs that there is "no substitute for a case-by-case inquiry into whether the legitimate interests of the authorities are demonstrably sufficient to circumscribe a teacher's speech." …

[F]ollowing Bishop's lead, we recognize "the 'basic educational mission'" of the university system, which authorizes "the use of 'reasonable restrictions' over in-class speech that [the State] could not censor outside the classroom." Along those same lines, we recognize Hazelwood Sch. Dist. v. Kuhlmeier's holding (endorsed in Bishop) that "educators do not offend the First Amendment by exercising editorial control over the style and content of student or professor speech in school-sponsored expressive activities so long as their actions are reasonably related to legitimate pedagogical concerns."

These factors cash out to the recognition that here, as in other First Amendment cases, we are called to balance the professors' interests against the State's. But the government's interests in this equation are rather circumscribed: editorial control is taken for granted, but restrictions must be reasonable and related to legitimate pedagogical concerns. In conducting this analysis, we consider Bishop's three general factors: the context of the speech; the state's "position as a public employer"; and the "strong predilection for academic freedom as an adjunct of the free speech rights of the First Amendment."

First, context. Here the context is the same as in Bishop: "the university classroom during specific in-class time." Bishop also noted that the "University's interest is most obvious when student complaints suggest apparent coercion—even when not intended by the professor." The word coercion, to be sure, has echoes of Establishment Clause precedents (particularly around the time of Bishop), but we think that concern is relevant here, too.

Florida's restrictions are, as the State admits, an attempt to force uniformity of thought on students by curtailing the free exchange of ideas in universities—the very environments traditionally regarded as laboratories for expression and truth seeking. Compelling certain beliefs by suppressing "individual thought and expression" is "especially" dangerous in the classroom context, "where the State acts against a background and tradition of thought and experiment that is at the center of our intellectual and philosophic tradition." Unlike in Bishop, where the University was furthering an anti-coercion goal, Florida here asserts the opposite interest—coercing university faculty (and by extension the students) into avoiding a certain set of ideas. Whether Florida is right or wrong about how dangerous these viewpoints are is irrelevant, at least to our inquiry as a court. "No matter how controversial the ideas, allowing the government to set the terms of the debate is poison, not antidote."

Second, we consider the State's interests as an employer. "Courts agree that the school's administration may at least establish the parameters of focus and general subject matter of curriculum." No doubt. And "[t]angential to the authority over its curriculum, there lies some authority over the conduct of teachers in and out of the classroom that significantly bears on the curriculum or that gives the appearance of endorsement by the university." This recognition, we think, significantly mitigates the risk that enforcing the First Amendment in the academic context would leave universities or other government authorities powerless to set overall curricular decisions or exclude speech that veers outside of well-understood parameters—personal tangents irrelevant to the subject matter, conspiracy theories, and the like.

Our own early cases support this same point. In one, we found no constitutional error when a university fired a professor whose speech "seriously impair[ed] … his effectiveness as an instructor." Ferguson v. Thomas (5th Cir. 1970). In another, we rejected a First Amendment challenge to a university's decision to deny tenure when the professor gave students two course credits for effectively taking only one course; awarded most students high grades for little work; made "untrue and misleading public statements"; and demonstrated a lack of "professionalism and maturity." Megill v. Bd. of Regents (5th Cir. 1976). And in Bishop, we again found no First Amendment violation when the university determined that a professor's tangential speech impeded his ability to properly teach physiology. These cases show that even when reserving space for academic freedom, university administrators (and perhaps other government officials too) have a legitimate interest in ensuring that a professor's in-class speech is both "germane" to the curriculum and "professionally competent."

Florida's justifications are not in line with those rationales. Rather than asserting an interest in promoting classroom efficiency or appropriately educating students, Florida says its "sovereign judgment" is that the forbidden viewpoints are "contrary to the State's most cherished ideals." But advancing cherished state ideals is a far cry from ordinary workplace management concerns, much less a legitimate pedagogical interest. What's more, even if many of these ideas are dead wrong, they are not at all irrelevant, at least in certain coursework. Florida itself seems to recognize this fact by allowing discussion of the viewpoints, even while barring their endorsement.

The State does assert one other justification for its law—an "interest in preventing invidious racial discrimination in public education." But as we have already held when considering the companion provision limiting private employers' speech, the Act does no such thing. Discrimination "generally means to treat differently," and "the Act does not regulate differential treatment"—it restricts speech that State political authorities find objectionable.

Preventing professors from positively discussing a viewpoint is not the same as regulating discrimination, which the State can certainly do. A professor who espouses a particular idea does not stand in the same shoes as one who ridicules a student, threatens a student, or targets a student. The Act does not limit (and does not purport to limit) actual discrimination of any kind. It instead assumes that mere exposure to what the State views as the wrong viewpoint is itself a serious harm. But a difference of opinion is not discrimination….

Third, we consider what we called in Bishop the "strong predilection for academic freedom." As we noted there (and here), scholarly freedom is "abundant[ly]" celebrated in First Amendment caselaw, and the "vigilant protection of constitutional freedoms is nowhere more vital than in the community of American schools." Indeed, our "Nation's future depends upon leaders trained through wide exposure to that robust exchange of ideas which discovers truth out of a multitude of tongues, rather than through any kind of authoritative selection."

Again, Bishop proves the point. True, that court also noted that "pronouncements about academic freedom" in other contexts "cannot be extrapolated to deny schools command of their own courses." To that end, we nod our heads along with Bishop as it recognizes that "academic freedom thrives not only on the independent and uninhibited exchange of ideas among teachers and students, but also, and somewhat inconsistently, on autonomous decisionmaking by the academy itself." Florida's Act, in contrast, removes any decisionmaking by the academy about the topics it bars. And the State's rationale is not limited—it would allow the legislature to block any speech on any topic for any reason….

The broader the restriction, and the more people whose speech it limits, the more scrutiny it must receive. Case-by-case restrictions on a single problematic professor's teaching are the bread and butter of First Amendment cases affirming the curricular authority of universities. But Florida tries to stretch those precedents to fit an entirely new context: not a university disciplining one professor for discrete misconduct, but a state government barring all professors from sharing politically disfavored ideas. {Bishop offers no holding about a state's authority to control curriculum; it protects a university's authority to control curriculum.} …

[D.] This Act's effect on academic freedom is compounded by its lack of clear definitions…. For example, when a professor assigns a reading for class that promotes one of the prohibited concepts, is that professor subjecting the student to "instruction that espouses, promotes, advances, inculcates, or compels such student … to believe" in the concept? What about when a professor hosts a guest speaker who presents only one side of the debate? {The defendants tell us that a professor would not violate the Act by hosting a guest speaker who promotes one of the eight concepts unless that professor endorses the guest's speech. This exacerbates rather than solves the problem, layering imprecision on imprecision.} Or how about a professor who teaches about one of the forbidden concepts using the Socratic method? Would those discussions be "objective"? The parties disagree on every front. And that is to say nothing of the lack of clear language in the individual concepts' definitions. Just to name one, what does "morally superior" encompass, and against whose perspective is it measured? …

And some excerpts from Judge Barbara Lagoa's very long dissent:

[A.] The professor-plaintiffs are state employees, and the speech at issue here occurs during their state-sponsored instruction in a course taken for credit by students enrolled in the State's public universities. According to the majority, a state can restrict a professor's speech so long as the restriction is "reasonable and related to legitimate pedagogical concerns." The majority also tells us that the State may legitimately prohibit a professor from discussing "irrelevant" material, "professionally [in]competent" material, "conspiracy theories, and the like."

The majority reassures that these are "well-understood parameters." But in practice, these "parameters" install a judge-made test that is unworkable and whose "policy-driven approach to the Constitution," reduces the First Amendment to a reflection of judicial preference, regardless of the State's interests in its classroom instruction….

To be clear, the First Amendment protects all viewpoints in the public square, whether they are conventional or controversial. But it does not compel all viewpoints to be worthy of state-sponsored endorsement….

The IFA concerns classroom instruction, allowing professors to discuss discriminatory concepts but not endorse or compel them…. [T]he state's interest as an employer outweighs a professor's interest in teaching his personal views.

[B.] Bishop … ought to end the matter…. Like Bishop, this case involves restrictions on a professor's speech in the classroom. And Bishop already held that, concerning classroom instruction, the State's interests outweigh those of the individual professor….

First, … [i]n Bishop, we noted that the University of Alabama legitimately feared that the professor's speech would have a "coercive effect" on his students: that students would feel compelled to show interest in the professor's religious views to obtain a good grade. The majority says this case is "[u]nlike" Bishop because there "the University was furthering an anti-coercion goal." Here, Florida supposedly "asserts the opposite interest—coercing [its] university faculty" to avoid "a certain set of ideas."

Respectfully, that distinction does not hold up. Bishop addressed coercion directed at students; we expressed no concern about any coercion suffered by a professor. In other words, the professor in Bishop, like the professor-plaintiffs here, was—in the majority's words—"coerc[ed]" to "avoid[] a certain set of ideas." That is why Bishop brought the suit. See Bishop, The majority's attempt to cast Florida's interest as somehow "opposite" of the interests in Bishop simply does not follow.

Second, … [o]n the question of relevance [of the professor's speech to the subject matter], Bishop assumed that the professor's religious views informed his professional viewpoint about exercise physiology. We held that the University could prevent him "from presenting his religious viewpoint during instructional time, even to the extent that it represents his professional opinion about his subject matter." We likewise recognized that the professor expressed "opinions about his work that happen[ed] to have a religious source."

Yet the University could still conclude—consistent with the First Amendment—that the professor's "opinions should not be represented in the courses he teaches at the University." The crux of Bishop is not, as the majority suggests, that religion is irrelevant to science, but that the State, as a public employer, may decide what is taught in its classrooms.

On the question of professional competence, we expressly refused to "gauge" whether the professor's views [in Bishop] were "well-founded." Our decision thus had nothing to do with "professional[] competen[ce]." In any event, a carveout for so-called "professionally incompetent" speech would permit, what the majority elsewhere condemns, "naked prohibition[s] on disfavored" viewpoints. Whether labeled "incompetent" or "unreasonable," the result is the same: the State may determine which viewpoints its employees may endorse during classroom instruction and which they may not.

Third, Bishop considered the First Amendment's "strong predilection for academic freedom as an adjunct of the free speech rights." … [But] Bishop held that a professor's "interest in academic freedom and free speech" does not ultimately "displace the University's interest inside the classroom." We noted that the state could not restrict a professor's speech "when he acts as an independent educator or researcher," but the state could restrict a professor's speech when he acts "as a course instructor."

[C.] In my view, the only legitimate difference here is that Bishop involved restrictions on one professor's speech while this case involves a statute of general application enacted by the Florida legislature. But that difference should not change the outcome of this case.

To start, Bishop did not treat "reasonable[ness]" as a license to rebalance interests in every new case. What made the restrictions "reasonable" was a single, concrete fact: "they appl[ied] only to [] classroom speech." That limitation carried the entire analysis. And when the IFA is read—as Bishop requires—"narrowly because [its restrictions] implicate First Amendment freedoms," its reach is likewise confined to classroom instruction.

Moreover, the Act expressly permits "discussion of the concepts listed," provided that any "training or instruction is given in an objective manner without endorsement of the concepts." It does not "ban" or reject any idea. The IFA bars Florida instructors from coercing students into believing they must embrace a prescribed viewpoint (possibly to receive good grades or preferential treatment in class, for instance). The same balance Bishop struck therefore resolves this case….

The majority nevertheless invokes U.S. v. National Treasury Employees Union (1995), for the proposition that "higher standards are necessary to vindicate broad, prophylactic restrictions on speech of a large number of employees …." … It is undisputed that, unlike Bishop, the IFA applies to the classroom instruction of all professors at public universities in Florida.

But why does that make a difference? NTEU involved speech that was unrelated to a public employee's duties at work. This case, by contrast, concerns speech by Florida's professors while they are performing their instructional duties. And Bishop expressly held that when an individual professor and his employer disagree "about a matter of content in the courses he teaches," the employer "must have the final say in such a dispute." There is little reason to think that balance changes when applied on a broader scale.

[D.] And the State's interests here are substantial in any event. The IFA prohibits instruction that people should be judged by their skin color, that people "should not attempt to treat" other races equally, and that other races "should be discriminated against." … [A] professor's endorsement of a particular viewpoint carries weight that can distort genuine academic exchange. As Bishop explained, a professor's words do not operate in a vacuum; they carry a "coercive effect" because the professor maintains authority over the students' grades.

Consider a professor who says that Hamas's October 7th attack on Israel was "exhilarating"8 and something to be celebrated and encouraged. If the professor made that statement as part of classroom instruction, would a Jewish student feel at liberty to wear his kippah or voice contrary views in the classroom, in a graded paper, or on an exam? Doubtful.

The majority's assurance that "a professor who espouses a particular idea does not stand in the same shoes as one who ridicules a student, threatens a student, or targets a student," does little to account for that reality. Florida acts well within its authority to curb professorial endorsements of that kind within its own classrooms….

[E.] Much of the majority's criticism of this dissent reduces to a supposed distinction between "a state's authority to control curriculum" and "a university's authority to control the curriculum."

But Florida law leaves the majority's university–State distinction with nowhere to stand. Florida's Constitution provides that "[t]here shall be a single state university system," that "[a] board of trustees shall administer each public university," and that "a board of governors shall govern the state university system." Florida lists each of the universities associated with the professor-plaintiﬀs as a "[s]tate university." And Florida declares that the "boards of trustees [of state universities] are a part of the executive branch of state government." Indeed, in the Eleventh Amendment context, Florida's grip on its public education system is so tight that its Boards of Trustees function as "arm[s] of the state." …

Taken together, these authorities make clear that Florida's public universities speak and act subject to the State's control and administration. Indeed, the IFA functions as the State's directive to its own employees, setting the standards that govern classroom instruction when, as Florida puts it, they teach "the State's curriculum, in the State's classrooms, on the State's time, in return for a State paycheck." And as this Court recognized in Bishop, the public employer has authority "to reasonably control the content of its curriculum, particularly that content imparted during class time."

{The Supreme Court has distinguished between a state's "attempts to direct the content of speech at private universities," where "[o]bvious First Amendment problems would arise," and a state's "attempts to direct the content of speech at public educational institutions," where "complicated First Amendment issues are presented because government is simultaneously both speaker and regulator." Univ. of Pennsylvania v. EEOC (1990).} …

[F.] The majority's rule, meant to avoid what it believes is the State's improper viewpoint discrimination, nonetheless endorses its own form of viewpoint discrimination. Of course, some amount of viewpoint discrimination is necessary to ensure the effective operation of any academic institution. The majority acknowledges as much, retaining vague carveouts—like "reasonable[ness]"—to distinguish between prohibitions that are and are not permissible under the First Amendment. The majority emphasizes that the boundary lines are "well-understood." It assures that "conspiracy theories" and "the like" will not be tolerated.

But these exceptions do not eliminate viewpoint discrimination. {Despite its assurance that it declines to "make any judgment about the value of the[se] ideas," the majority does exactly that. By its own account, it believes "universities or other government authorities" may "exclude speech that veers outside of well-understood parameters." But what does "veer outside of well-understood parameters" mean and why must it be this majority who decides?

Those questions go unanswered. Rather, the majority simultaneously labels certain categories of speech as properly excludable while insisting that "it is not our place" to judge the value of contested viewpoints.}

Put diﬀerently, the majority recognizes that someone must set the limits of what can be taught at public universities. And through its carveouts, the majority shifts that decision-making authority from the people to federal judges.

The test now is: Does the presiding judge believe that the professor's viewpoint is within the range of permissible views? … The majority's rule still allows "naked prohibition[s] on disfavored speech," but authorizes that viewpoint discrimination under exceptions created, interpreted, and enforced by the judiciary.

Note that my students and I filed an amicus brief on behalf of the Academic Freedom Alliance, supporting the challenge to the law.

 

The post Eleventh Circuit Panel Strikes Down (by 2-1 Vote) Florida University Professor Speech Restrictions appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Nigel Farage Resigns His Parliamentary Seat—and Is Standing for Election Again
articles.content:
奈杰尔·法拉奇是英国改革党的领袖，也是美国当选总统唐纳德·特朗普的盟友。他于周二辞去了克拉克顿选区国会议员职务，引发了该选区的补选。但法拉奇的政治生涯远未结束。他表示将竞选刚刚辞去的同一席位。

法拉奇的辞职源于外界对他过去几年未申报财务捐款的质疑。其中包括2024年参选前不久，泰国籍加密货币亿万富翁克里斯托弗·哈伯恩向他支付了500万英镑（约670万美元）。据《卫报》报道，哈伯恩在过去七年间向法拉奇的政党捐款超过2200万英镑（约2900万美元）。《星期日泰晤士报》也披露，英国贵族乔治·科特雷尔曾为法拉奇的部分政治活动提供资金支持，包括社交媒体、工作人员、安保、旅行和住宿等方面。然而，法拉奇只申报了部分与旅行相关的捐款。

法拉奇在周二的视频声明中表示：“请让我把话说清楚。我没有做错任何事。我完全没有违反任何法律。我没有滥用公共资金。”“担任议员的头两年，我的个人议员开支为零……当然，议会有关于成员行为规范的规则。我相信我完全遵守了这些规则。”

根据议会行为准则，议员必须申报任何“可能被他人合理地认为会影响其行为或言论”的利益。在选举前一年内收到的、与“政治活动”“在任何方面”相关的礼物，必须登记在利益登记册上。“纯个人”礼物可豁免申报，例如“来自伴侣或家人的”礼物，但准则指出“应考虑送礼者的可能动机以及礼物的用途”。准则还指出，“如有任何疑问，应登记该利益”。

今年5月，议会标准监督机构就法拉奇未申报哈伯恩500万英镑捐款一事展开调查。法拉奇最初表示，这笔钱是用于支付安保费用的个人礼物，因此无需申报。随后有报道称，法拉奇以现金购买了价值140万英镑（约190万美元）的房产，但改革党表示“房产的要约和购买程序在该笔捐款之前就已开始”。法拉奇后来改变了说法，称这笔捐款是“奖励”，因为他为脱欧进行了宣传活动。

法拉奇不愿将自己的政治命运交给议会调查人员，而是决定交给他的选民。他周二表示：“克拉克顿的人民应该成为我行为的审判者。这将是一场人民对建制派的补选。”

但这背后可能还有其他动机。据《卫报》报道，议会标准调查可能在补选期间暂停。根据程序议定书，如果“议员在调查进行期间停止担任议员，委员将暂停调查”。然而，法拉奇只能暂时逃避审查。议会的规则规定，如果“议员重新当选”，调查将继续进行，甚至可能“在该议员未能重返议会的情况下”继续进行。

包括保守党和工党在内的多个政党已宣布不会在补选中提名候选人。保守党领袖凯米·巴德诺赫指责法拉奇在“发脾气”，并表示他们“不会在法拉奇制造的虚假补选中派出候选人，以转移人们对正在发生的事的注意力”。自由民主党领袖埃德·戴维则呼吁政府在调查结束前阻止法拉奇辞职。目前唯一确认参选的其他候选人是喜剧演员乔纳森·大卫·哈维，又名“垃圾桶脸伯爵”，他自称“独立太空战士”，戴着一个巨大的垃圾桶形状头盔。

这场“人民对建制派”的补选无疑会为法拉奇带来大量媒体关注，他很可能是希望借此转移人们对其财务问题的关注。如果他最终获胜——这很可能发生——他2024年以46.2%的得票率当选，且改革党的支持率此后只增不减——他可能会利用这次胜利作为自己没有做错任何事的证据。但克拉克顿的选民投票给他，并不意味着他没有违反议会标准。

这是英国政治中一个更广泛趋势的一部分。政客们越来越愿意将宪法惯例视为可以操纵的工具。工党计划在曼彻斯特前市长安迪·伯纳姆当选为议会中的马克菲尔德选区议员后，将他空降为首相，这是一个更为严重的例子。不过，法拉奇辞去自己的席位并重新举行选举以转移标准调查的注意力，也是一个可怕的先例。

补选当然也很昂贵。虽然确切费用目前尚不清楚，但从2010年到2016年，补选的平均费用为228,964英镑（约30.6万美元）。今天的成本无疑已经上涨，虽然改革党已提出支付补选费用，但这很可能不被允许（你不能让一位候选人支付一场自由公正选举的费用）。因此，英国纳税人很可能要为这场政治秀买单。

问题不在于法拉奇能否在克拉克顿再次赢得选票，而在于一位现任议员是否可以在不申报的情况下接受数百万美元的礼物。他可以将审查称为“建制派”的攻击，称对方使用“卑鄙手段”，也许还能利用这场补选来巩固支持，并最终成为首相。但来自克拉克顿的个人授权无法回答法拉奇是否遵守规则这一基本问题。

本文首次发表于Reason.com
articles.summary:
Nigel Farage, the leader of Reform U.K. and an ally of U.S. President Donald Trump, resigned as the member of Parliament (M.P.) for Clacton on Tuesday, triggering a by-election in the seat. But Farage's political career is far from over. He has said he will run for the same seat he just vacated.

The resignation follows speculation concerning undeclared financial gifts Farage has received over the past few years, including a 5 million pound (around $6.7 million) payment from Christopher Harborne, a Thailand-based cryptocurrency billionaire, shortly before he stood for election in 2024. Harborne has given Farage's party more than 22 million pounds (around $29 million) over the past seven years, according to The Guardian. The Sunday Times also reported that George Cottrell, a British aristocrat who served prison time in the U.S. for wire fraud, provided funding for parts of Farage's political operation, including social media, staff, security, travel, and accommodation. However, Farage only declared some travel-related donations.

"Let me be absolutely clear. I have done nothing wrong. I have not broken the law in any way at all. I have not misused public money," Farage said in a video statement on Tuesday. "For the first two years of being an M.P., my personal M.P. expenses are zero….And yes, of course, Parliament has its rules about how members ought to behave. And I believe I've absolutely obeyed those rules."

Under Parliament's code of conduct, M.P.s must declare any benefit that "might reasonably be thought by others to influence [their] actions or words." Any gifts received in the year preceding their election, and that relate "in any way" to their "political activities," must be on their register of interests. "Purely personal" gifts are exempt, such as gifts "from partners or family members," but the code states that "both the possible motive of the giver and the use to which the gift is to be put should be considered." The code states that "if there is any doubt, the benefit should be registered."

In May, the parliamentary standards watchdog launched an investigation into Farage for not declaring the 5 million pound gift from Harborne. Farage originally said that the money was a personal gift to cover his security costs, and so he did not need to declare it. Subsequently, it emerged that Farage purchased a 1.4 million pound (about $1.9 million) property in cash, but the Reform party says the "offer and purchase process for the property commenced before the gift." Farage later changed his tune, calling the gift a "reward" for campaigning for Brexit.

Rather than leaving his political fate up to parliamentary investigators, he's leaving it to his constituents. "The people of Clacton should be the judges of my actions," Farage said on Tuesday. "This will be a people vs. the establishment by-election."

But there are probably other motives at play. According to The Guardian, the parliamentary standards investigation may be paused during the by-election. Indeed, the Procedural Protocol states that if "the Member otherwise ceases to be a Member while an investigation is in progress, the Commissioner will suspend their investigation." However, Farage will only be able to avoid scrutiny for so long. The investigation would continue if "the Member is re-elected," per Parliament's rules, and could even continue "if the Member is not returned to Parliament."

Various political parties, including the Conservatives and Labour, have announced they will not be nominating candidates for the by-election. Kemi Badenoch, the leader of the Conservatives, accused Farage of "having a hissy fit" and said that they would "not be standing a candidate in the fake by-election that Farage is causing to distract people from what is happening." Ed Davey, the leader of the Liberal Democrats, called for the government to block Farage's resignation until the investigation is over. The only other candidate confirmed to stand is Jonathan David Harvey, a.k.a. Count Binface, a comedy candidate and self-described "independent space warrior" who wears a large bin-shaped helmet.

This "people vs. the establishment" by-election will undoubtedly give Farage plenty of media attention, and he is probably hoping it will take the heat off his finances. In the event that he does win, which is likely—he won with 46.2 percent of the vote in 2024, and his party's popularity has only grown since then—he will probably use that win as evidence that he has done nothing wrong. But just because the people of Clacton might vote for him in a by-election, it does not mean he did not break parliamentary standards.

This is part of a broader trend in British politics. Politicians are increasingly willing to treat constitutional conventions as tools to be gamed. The Labour Party's plan to parachute former Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham into the premiership following his election to the Makerfield seat in Parliament is a more egregious example. Still, Farage resigning his own seat to rerun an election to distract from a standards investigation is an awful precedent to set, too.

By-elections are also, of course, expensive. While the exact cost is currently unknown, from 2010 to 2016, the average cost of by-elections for taxpayers was 228,964 pounds (about $306,000). The cost today has undoubtedly increased, and while Reform has offered to pay for the costs of the by-election, this will likely not be allowed (you cannot have a free and fair election that is paid for by one of the candidates standing). Therefore, British taxpayers will likely have to foot the bill for this political theater.

The issue is not whether Farage can win votes in Clacton again, but whether a sitting M.P. can receive millions of dollars in gifts without declaring them. He can dismiss the scrutiny as an "establishment" attack using "foul means," and perhaps be able to use this by-election to shore up his support and eventually become prime minister. But a personal mandate from Clacton would not answer the basic question of whether Farage followed the rules.

The post Nigel Farage Resigns His Parliamentary Seat—and Is Standing for Election Again appeared first on Reason.com.
